text: We are young and therefore inexperienced and so we consult our parents on almost everything including our studies
comments: This fits decision-making because it explains how youth make choices. I coded it as nuanced rather than fully restrictive because the statement emphasizes consultation, not complete lack of voice.

text: Our parents have much more experience and much wiser than us
comments: This supports the norm that elders should guide decisions. It is a nuanced decision-making norm because youth are not saying they have no role, but that elders should lead.

text: We follow our parents and learn from them
comments: This reflects deference to parents in decision-making. I coded it as nuanced because it shows guidance and obedience rather than explicit coercion.

text: They are our elders and our fathers are the household heads and therefore we must respect them
comments: This confirms hierarchical authority, especially male authority, in the household. I coded it as confirming restricted decision-making because fathers are explicitly described as heads.

text: I am the head of my family and work hard to support them. So I have the right to take decisions for my family
comments: This shows individual authority rather than restricted agency. I coded it as opposing restricted decision-making because the speaker claims strong power to decide.

text: Once we start earning and contributing to the family expenses then we will be able to have a say in family matters
comments: This links decision-making power to income contribution. It is nuanced because agency is conditional rather than absent.

text: But we must consult our parents for all major decisions in life
comments: This clearly confirms limited youth autonomy over major life choices.

text: Once we get a job then we will get the power to make decisions
comments: This is decision-making tied to employment status, so I coded it as nuanced rather than full restriction.

text: Education is important to progress in life
comments: This is a broad life aspiration linked to education, but no specific occupation is named.

text: Education is important to live a good life
comments: This expresses a general aspiration for a better life through education.

text: It will help us get a good government job
comments: This is a clear occupational aspiration for formal salaried work.

text: Nowadays technical knowledge is more important for getting a government job
comments: This links aspiration to modern technical skills and employment, so I coded it as modern occupational aspiration.

text: Education will help us improve the economic condition of our families
comments: This is an aspiration for improved family well-being through education, not one specific profession.

text: Education also improves our personality and increases our confidence
comments: This frames education as self-development and confidence-building.

text: Education will make us doctors, engineers and police officers
comments: This is a direct set of occupational aspirations for respected salaried professions.

text: No one can dupe us if we are educated
comments: Education is linked to empowerment and protection in life, not a specific job.

text: If we are well educated then we will get job opportunities in big cities too
comments: This expresses aspiration for urban employment opportunities through education.

text: Boys complete their 12th while girls study up to class 10
comments: This shows a clear gender difference in attainment. I coded it as a wide gap because girls’ schooling stops earlier.

text: Both boys and girls must be allowed to complete their bachelors plus B.Ed (Bachelor’s in education) and must also be provided training in computers
comments: This is a clear aspiration for higher education and employability for both sexes.

text: We cannot do anything if we don’t know how to work on computers
comments: This reflects aspiration toward modern skills needed for contemporary work.

text: Unless we are graduates we will not be able to do any work besides agriculture
comments: This suggests limited options without education, creating a strong aspiration-attainment gap.

text: B.Ed will help us become teachers
comments: This is a direct occupational aspiration.

text: We need to be graduates to get a government/private job
comments: This ties higher education directly to formal employment aspirations.

text: Yes, parents show same commitment to educating their daughters as their sons
comments: This is a direct statement in favor of equal treatment in education.

text: Education is important for marriage too
comments: This suggests daughters’ education is valued, though partly through marriageability. I still coded it as pro-equality because it supports educating girls.

text: It will be good for the family if both sons and daughters are educated
comments: This supports equal educational investment in sons and daughters.

text: No, parents are not equally committed; if they have to choose between the two then they prefer to educate their sons
comments: This directly confirms unequal commitment favoring boys.

text: They are forced by their economic condition to make this choice
comments: Economic pressure is described as the reason for choosing boys over girls, widening girls’ attainment gap.

text: College is too far and parents consider it unsafe for their girls to commute
comments: This clearly confirms mobility restrictions affecting girls’ education.

text: It is difficult to find a groom for girls who are too educated
comments: This reflects a norm against very educated girls in marriage, showing inequality in how education is valued by gender.

text: Most of us know all agricultural activities
comments: This indicates broad agricultural knowledge among boys.

text: We help our fathers in the field and hence know all agricultural activities – sowing, irrigating, harvesting and everything else
comments: This is clearly about learning agricultural knowledge through participation with fathers.

text: There is no higher secondary school in our village
comments: Lack of local schooling is a structural barrier that widens the education gap.

text: There is no college here
comments: Lack of college access strongly constrains attainment.

text: Poor roads
comments: Poor infrastructure limits access to continued education.

text: Poor financial condition
comments: Poverty is a major barrier to continuing education.

text: Need to earn and help the family
comments: Pressure to earn diverts youth away from study.

text: Early marriage
comments: Early marriage is named as a key reason education ends.

text: Lack of interest in education
comments: This is a barrier, but more individual and less structural, so I coded it as a smaller gap.

text: Poor economic condition
comments: Identified as one of the two most important reasons education ends.

text: Lack of higher secondary school and college in the village
comments: This is explicitly ranked as a top structural reason for ending education.

text: I tried to get a teacher’s job but failed since I did not have a B.Ed.
comments: This contains a clear teacher aspiration. Because failure is central too, it also reflects a gap, but I am coding the row by the aspiration stated.

text: failed since I did not have a B.Ed.
comments: This is the attainment barrier attached to the teaching aspiration.

text: I wanted to be a police but ended up working in agriculture
comments: Clear occupational aspiration for policing.

text: ended up working in agriculture
comments: Indicates a strong mismatch between desired and actual occupation.

text: A friend of mine wanted to be a police officer but instead is rolling bidis today
comments: Another explicit occupational aspiration.

text: instead is rolling bidis today
comments: The actual bidi work shows a substantial aspiration-attainment gap.

text: It is difficult to fulfill our career dream unless we get a good college in our village
comments: This directly links failure to attain career dreams to lack of local college access.

text: Most young men end up doing agricultural work
comments: This reflects a common livelihood outcome in traditional agriculture.

text: Some have started small business
comments: This is direct evidence of business-oriented livelihood aspiration/trajectory.

text: Most are also making bidis like us
comments: Bidi-making is a livelihood activity, though not clearly an aspiration; still relevant to post-school life.

text: Some are doing different kinds of labor work
comments: This describes common livelihood paths after schooling.

text: There is no decent employment in our village and we need to migrate
comments: Lack of local jobs creates a strong aspiration-attainment gap and pushes migration.

text: I want to start a business
comments: Direct business aspiration.

text: We should be able to get a good government/private job
comments: Clear aspiration for formal wage employment.

text: Those with lot of land can do agriculture
comments: This points to agriculture as a possible path where land resources exist.

text: We should move to bigger towns for better job prospects
comments: Migration is framed as a workaround for limited local opportunity, so I coded this as a smaller gap rather than complete blockage.

text: Seed driller
comments: Mention of a new technology indicates awareness of innovation.

text: Rotavator
comments: Another cited technology showing knowledge of innovation.

text: Tractor
comments: Mechanization is recognized as part of local agricultural change.

text: Thresher
comments: Another item showing awareness of improved technology.

text: Seeds like Lokmanya
comments: New seed types indicate knowledge of varietal innovation.

text: Women hardly work with machines
comments: This supports the norm that mechanized farming is male-coded.

text: Women don’t work with machines
comments: Confirms that key technologies are seen as men’s domain.

text: Machines save time and labor
comments: Technology is valued for efficiency, showing positive perception of innovation.

text: It reduces our parents’ work burden
comments: This speaks to work reallocation through technology; not strictly gendered but tied to labor patterns.

text: We get time to study
comments: Technology is linked to better educational opportunity.

text: Now our parents also get more time to make bidis which gets additional income
comments: This points to livelihood improvement and income aspiration.

text: We need less seeds for same production when we sow with seed driller
comments: This shows understanding of the technical benefit of a new practice.

text: It also saves money on fertilizer since we need less fertilizer when we sow with seed driller
comments: This demonstrates specific knowledge about innovation benefits.

text: Women have the same opportunities as men but they prefer to work manually
comments: This nuance says opportunities are equal in principle, but uptake differs.

text: Women are not comfortable with new technologies especially machines; they believe machines are meant for men
comments: This directly confirms the norm that mechanized farming belongs to men.

text: Women don’t do much agricultural work here; they mostly make bidis
comments: This confirms a gendered division of labor, with women associated with bidi work rather than farm work.

text: Women do agricultural activities under the guidance of men
comments: This indicates women’s agricultural participation is subordinate to men’s authority.

text: Young women don’t do much agricultural work here
comments: Less participation means less direct learning and experimentation opportunity.

text: When people started migrating for better work then they learnt many new things and they brought back all this new information to the village
comments: This shows information diffusion through migration.

text: because people started migrating so there was shortage of labor in the village which forced many people to adopt labor saving technologies
comments: Explains how structural change led to innovation uptake.

text: Technology spread when people saw its benefits
comments: Shows observational learning and spread of innovation.

text: Technology and machines have become our need today
comments: Strong positive perception of technology adoption.

text: There is more information access today as compared to ten years ago
comments: Direct statement that access to information has improved.

text: Yes, because women cannot do all agricultural activities
comments: This directly confirms the idea that women are less capable farmers.

text: Men do all agricultural activities but women only do some like sowing and harvesting
comments: This confirms a clear gender division of labor.

text: We cannot expect women to drive tractors
comments: This is a strong statement that mechanized farming belongs to men.

text: Women are busy with household work and making bidis
comments: Confirms women’s domestic and bidi roles as reasons for different farm tasks.

text: Women don’t do physically demanding activities like land preparation
comments: This reflects a gendered division based on perceived physical ability.

text: It will be extremely difficult and they (young women) will not want to do such a thing themselves
comments: This confirms strong restrictions on women independently growing and selling crops.

text: Their parents will not allow them
comments: Direct confirmation of restricted agency.

text: They will not get the support of their family members and they will never go against their family
comments: Family pressure and obedience norms stigmatize nonconforming female behavior.

text: They don’t have full knowledge to be able to grow themselves and they have no market knowledge to be able to sell
comments: This explicitly frames women as lacking knowledge for production and selling.

text: Even young men need to take their parent’s permission
comments: This shows men’s agency is also somewhat conditional on parents’ authority.

text: But we will not face any problem in growing since we know the entire process
comments: Indicates young men’s confidence in their agricultural knowledge.

text: But we will face problem in selling in the regional market since the village lacks transport facility
comments: This is a practical barrier, but not a complete blockage, so I coded it as a smaller gap.

text: But we are aware enough to be able to do it on our own
comments: Confirms male self-perception of competence and knowledge.

text: The household head takes such decisions
comments: Direct confirmation that authority over plots lies with the household head.

text: Mostly our fathers decide who will do what
comments: This confirms male elder control over labor decisions.

text: Generally the household head distributes work amongst the male members of the house
comments: This indicates gendered assignment of productive roles among men.

text: This is not a very big issue since entire family pitches in to grow a good crop
comments: Opposes a strict individual plot/labor division by stressing collective family effort.

text: We don’t have separate plots for men and women
comments: Opposes a strict separation of plots by gender.

text: What is the harm in a man helping on his wife’s plot?
comments: This explicitly challenges rigid gender divisions in agricultural labor.

text: Both husband and wife should help out on each other’s land
comments: Supports shared labor rather than gender-exclusive plots.

text: All family members will help each other
comments: Again opposes rigid role separation by emphasizing cooperation.

text: It is not about man or woman; it is family property/land and the family works together
comments: This opposes strict gender distinctions in plot responsibility.

text: Men usually do the physically intensive work while women do the easier work like harvesting
comments: Confirms a gendered labor division by physical intensity.

text: Whoever has time helps out on the land; it is our main source of livelihood and everyone makes an effort to grow a good crop
comments: This softens the strict labor division by showing practical cooperation.

text: Yes, women need to consult their husband
comments: Direct confirmation that women do not independently decide about a new crop.

text: Women will consult other family members in the absence of their husband
comments: Women’s decision-making remains dependent on family permission.

text: Women will need their family’s permission and especially that of their husbands’
comments: Strong confirmation of restricted female agency.

text: Men will ask other elder family members
comments: Men also consult elders, but not in as restrictive a way as women.

text: Men may or may not ask their wives
comments: This is nuanced because wives may be consulted but are not always central.

text: Men will also consult their farmer friends
comments: This reflects wider information networks for men.

text: He will allow her to join the group
comments: Opposes strong mobility restriction by allowing women’s group participation.

text: He will not mind her joining such a group
comments: This also indicates acceptance of women’s participation outside the home.

text: He will not have any problem in joining such a group since she herself is also growing vegetables
comments: Shows conditional acceptance of women’s collective activity.

text: Adam will allow her to go
comments: Indicates permissive attitude toward women selling produce.

text: Women can go but they prefer not to
comments: Mobility is possible but shaped by preference/custom, so nuanced.

text: She can go if she wants to
comments: This supports women’s independent choice to go to market.

text: Yes, he will ask her less for help now that she is busy with her own work
comments: Suggests some recognition of women’s independent workload, softening rigid labor expectations.

text: Since she has a busy schedule so he will not expect much help from her
comments: Again indicates some accommodation of women’s own activities.

text: He will let her concentrate on her work
comments: This reflects support for women’s economic activity.

text: She also has to do all the household work and so she will have no time now to help her husband
comments: Confirms women’s domestic burden remains assumed.

text: It will depend upon the mentality of the community people
comments: Social reaction is mixed, so I coded stigma as nuanced rather than uniformly condemning.

text: Some may praise her for taking up the family responsibilities
comments: Positive perception of woman supporting family suggests acceptance of expanded roles.

text: some may not like her bold step
comments: This indicates some stigma remains around women’s public market role.

text: people will admire her for taking care of her family
comments: This praises a woman’s economic contribution, suggesting a positive equality-related shift.

text: Mark’s reputation will not be affected
comments: Opposes stigma against men helping at home.

text: Community elders will try to reason with him since it is a man’s responsibility to earn for his family
comments: This shows some disapproval rooted in provider norms, but not total rejection, so nuanced stigma.

text: People will not have any problem if both husband and wife are working; but in this case there is a role reversal which goes against the norms of the society
comments: Clear statement that shared work is acceptable, but role reversal is still socially sensitive.

text: People will tell him that he will now be controlled by his wife since she is the family breadwinner
comments: This directly confirms stigma against men when wives become breadwinners.

text: Mark will be very happy
comments: This is a supportive reaction to female enterprise success.

text: He will now have more money to enjoy
comments: This is still positive toward wife’s earnings, though more self-interested.

text: Mark may now want to join his wife’s business and make it more prosperous
comments: This points to enterprise growth and shared business aspiration.

text: He will praise her and encourage her to do better
comments: This shows support for women’s economic success and agency.

text: Both men and women should be meted out the same treatment by society
comments: Direct definition of gender equality.

text: They should both be given equal respect
comments: Directly supports equal status for men and women.

text: Both should have equal say in their marriage decisions
comments: Clear support for equal decision-making in marriage.

text: Parents should consult both boys and girls in every decision that concerns them
comments: Strong equality statement regarding youth consultation.

text: Both should be allowed to graduate before getting married
comments: Supports equal educational opportunity and delayed marriage for both sexes.

text: Both should be given equal love and care in the family
comments: Direct support for equal treatment in the family.

text: Girls should also have a right to speak their mind
comments: This explicitly supports girls’ voice and expression.

text: Both should have freedom of thought and expression
comments: Directly articulates equality in rights and expression.

text: They will discuss and decide
comments: This opposes restricted female decision-making because the couple decides jointly.

text: It will depend upon the need of the family
comments: Decisions are shared but still shaped by family need rather than pure individual choice.

text: David is more likely to sell the vegetables
comments: This confirms men’s stronger association with selling.

text: David will have to go since the market is too far and women cannot go by themselves
comments: Women’s independent mobility is restricted by distance and norms.

text: It is not possible for women to go to the market since there is no transport
comments: Transport barriers combine with gender norms to restrict women’s mobility.

text: Women also have to do the household work and there is no time for them to go to the market
comments: Household burden is used to explain why women do not sell.

text: Yes, David will agree to make the changes
comments: This suggests women’s innovation ideas can be accepted.

text: They will make the change in a small portion of their land
comments: Indicates openness to trying innovation in a cautious way.

text: David will trust Diana and will implement the changes
comments: This is a positive sign of recognizing a woman’s knowledge and judgment.

text: Women will never go against their husbands’ wishes
comments: This strongly confirms restricted female agency.

text: This is a male dominated society and a man always prevails
comments: This directly explains why women face difficulty using inherited assets independently.

text: Woman may fight but will not have the courage to defy her husband
comments: This confirms women’s constrained agency under patriarchal norms.

text: Such are the norms and traditions of our society
comments: This invokes community norms as the basis for women not opposing husbands.

text: Society has given a lot of power to men and this is a patriarchal society and men can do whatever they want
comments: This confirms stronger male control over assets and decisions.

text: It is his inheritance and therefore he doesn’t need anyone’s permission to whatever he wants with it
comments: Men are described as having unquestioned rights over inherited assets.

text: Men don’t have to take their wives’ permission
comments: This confirms asymmetry in household decision-making power.

text: Yes, they can walk alone comfortably
comments: Opposes strong restrictions on young women’s local mobility.

text: Many young women go to the market
comments: Indicates women’s public presence is normal in the local market.

text: Girls go to school via the marketplace
comments: This normalizes girls’ movement in public space.

text: There are lot of people in the market at any given time and therefore it is safe for them
comments: Safety is cited as a reason why women can move there.

text: No, she cannot go alone to the market
comments: This confirms a mobility restriction for young married women.

text: She needs to be accompanied by her husband or some other family member
comments: Married women’s mobility is conditional on accompaniment.

text: Our social customs don’t permit young married women to go alone to the market
comments: Clear confirmation of norm-based mobility restriction.

text: It will be hard for a woman to sell in the local market since it is dominated by men
comments: This confirms women’s difficulty accessing market space because of male dominance.

text: Women don’t have the time to sell since they have to take care of all the work at home and then make bidis to order
comments: Domestic and bidi work burden limits women’s market participation.

text: It is not safe for women to sell in the market
comments: Safety concerns are directly cited as a barrier to women’s market work.

text: There are no security threats within the village
comments: This supports the idea that within-village mobility is relatively free.

text: Women can move around freely within the village
comments: This explicitly opposes a strong restriction on women’s movement within the village.

text: Many young men have migrated from our village in search of better job opportunities
comments: Migration for jobs indicates local employment opportunities are inadequate.

text: It is never easy in a new place for anyone and people go from here with limited money to tide over the interim period and hence it is important to get a job fast
comments: This describes the risky gap between aspiration for better work and actual conditions in migration.

text: If the money runs out before they can find a job then they may have to return unless there is a relative to provide free boarding
comments: This is a direct structural barrier to attaining migration-based work goals.

text: There are no job prospects here and such a person will either do farm activities or engage in bidi making
comments: This clearly states the lack of local opportunities after return.

text: What can people say? No one will return unless they have failed to find a job for whatever reason
comments: There is implied stigma around return migration as failure, but expressed indirectly, so I coded it as nuanced.

text: People migrate with dreams of a better future and they try their best to find a decent job
comments: This directly states a broad employment aspiration motivating migration.

text: But sometimes people return with money and knowledge too
comments: Return migration can generate capital and knowledge for future enterprise or livelihood improvement.

text: Girls get married at 17-18 while boys at 20-21
comments: Different marriage ages reflect unequal expectations for girls and boys.

text: Parents of the prospective bride and groom take the decision
comments: Confirms family-led marriage decision-making.

text: But parents consult us before taking the final decision
comments: Youth are consulted, but final decision is not theirs, so nuanced.

text: But parents may or may not ask the girls
comments: This confirms stronger restriction on girls’ voice in marriage decisions.

text: But they consult their boys
comments: Boys are more likely to be consulted, showing unequal but not absolute agency.

text: Our parents are experienced and therefore it is good that they take such decisions
comments: Supports parent-led marriage decisions as a norm.

text: But they should also always consult their daughters
comments: This adds a clear pro-equality element to marriage decision-making.

text: Parents should allow their children to choose their own life partners (love marriage)
comments: This opposes strict parental control over marriage choice.

text: Women have their first child at 20
comments: This states the normative timing of women’s childbearing.

text: Women have their first child within two years of marriage
comments: Suggests a social expectation of early childbearing after marriage.

text: I think 20 is the right age for a woman to have babies
comments: This supports a particular age norm, though not all participants agree, so nuanced.

text: I think women should get married at 23 and have their first child at 25
comments: Supports later marriage and later childbearing, which often aligns with better autonomy and preparedness.

text: After 23
comments: Statement of preferred later age for first childbirth.

text: Between 23-25 years of age
comments: Clear preference for later first birth.

text: Both husband and wife need time to understand each other before starting a family
comments: This supports a more thoughtful and equal approach to family formation.

text: Then they will be better prepared to raise kids
comments: Indicates value placed on readiness and planning before childbirth.

text: Yes, but they have to consult their husband
comments: Women do participate in childbearing decisions, but consultation with husbands is expected.

text: Both take such decisions together
comments: This supports joint reproductive decision-making.

text: Sometimes family puts a lot of pressure on them to have children
comments: Family pressure limits fully free reproductive decision-making.

text: But they must also assess their economic situation before starting a family
comments: This shows some rational planning by the couple, so nuanced rather than fully restrictive.

text: We need to have a higher secondary school and a college in our village
comments: This is a strong aspiration for better educational infrastructure.

text: Both men and women should get equal work opportunities
comments: Direct support for gender equality in employment.

text: Both should get equal education opportunities
comments: Direct support for gender equality in education.

text: We want better employment opportunities in our village
comments: Broad aspiration for improved local employment.

text: We want our village to be a neat and clean place to live in
comments: This is a general aspiration for better living conditions in the village.
